children.text: I could fill the heel of your boot academically because-
Boy, the battery of this came out.
No, I say that I hope that one day I can fill the heel of your boot academically. [laughter]
Oh, come on.

I've written some short stories and stuff, remember that I promised to send you and I brought them to you Tio, so you could read at your leisure. Some poesías, de pos, you know my mother was always quoting Longfellow and "learn to labor and to wait" and-- Who was the other one?
¿cómo se llamaba el "We are architects of fate working within these walls of time"?
She loved Longfellow.
She loved Longfellow and she really instilled- [video cut]

Back to something you asked me, why did I work so hard to get an education? When I finished high school the prospects I had then was working at a shoe store, or a dry good store selling shoes, and I hated that, and it certainly did not lead anywhere. I got the chance

to go two years at college and, of course, that flooded my appetite. I knew that most of my other school mates went on to get a degree. The best I could do was get a job at the Brownsville Herald making $11.40 a week and even though at that time that was a little more than is today,

it wasn't very much. [coughs] In 1940 when things got really- when the the war really got started in, in Europe. Pan American Airways started arming planes for Great Britain. I got a job there at $15 a week but I was still at that time the Brownsville Herald and The Monitor of McAllen, and the Morning Star

were bought all by a man named Hubert Hudson and all became-- They continued to publish separately but this Sunday paper was done in Brownsville and was for all of them. I still had a job there Saturdays and Sundays, I wrote a great deal for the Brownsville Herald.

In fact, a lot of the articles I wrote on Mexican culture that were used in the early Charros Days were cannibalized and printed, and reprinted later. My salary rose to about $15, I was getting $15 over there, and at that time [coughs] I got a job singing my own songs on the radio that I was paid

the magnificent sum-- I loved to mention that, $1 a minute for 15 minutes a week, so I really was making for what people like me would make at that time- a lot of money, $45 a week. Though inflation was on, I still felt that I was at a dead end. So this is something that some people thought

I was crazy to do because the job I had at with PanAm was a war job, therefore I was exempt from the draft. There were a number of reasons, including the fact that some of my friends, Matías Serrata, who played the guitar with me, was killed in France. Este muchacho, se me olvida el nombre,

Miguel Morán, died after action in Attu, and I began to get a guilt feeling but also I want to get out of Brownsville because it was a dead end as far as education was concerned. I just quit the job with Pan American and I could not volunteer because at that time this was in '44 already,

they didn't want people in the Navy, they didn't want people in the Air Force, they wanted people with rifles. They weren't called grunts at that time but that was what they wanted so they told me, "Go home, we'll call you," and I worked with The Herald full-time for a living, they took me.

In a way it was very good for me because somebody who had been reading too many detective books saw that I was a newspaper reporter and they put me in the CID, the civil, or Criminal Investigation Division. Now, those were supposed to be detectives. There was one other reporter from California,

all the rest were former highway patrolmen, detectives, and we were supposed to be trained in law enforcement. Of course, I washed out on that, and so at that time they sent me to Seattle, and at that time the war ended anyway, so by the time I ended up in Japan, in Nagoya, the war was over.

Again, a lucky break, they were starting a newspaper in the base, the replacement center, and there were several people there. There were two men, one from New York, one from San Francisco, both of them are Jewish extraction, both of them Left-wing radicals hasta las cachas,

one of them was a member of the Communist Party. They were looking over, they saw my name, and they had read a book by Hart Stilwell, he had been my editor who had read my book. He had not been offended by what I say in the novel about the Rangers,

his father was a Texas ranger, but he was enraged about what I said about Dobie. We fell out on that, he said, "The old man would do anything you wanted to," "Yes, Massa would do anything for me, wouldn't he?" So we had a falling out on that and he never was interested in that.

He published a book called Border City in which he was a radical reporter very much like me, in it. These people went to see whom they could pick to work in the paper and they asked me where I was from, I said, "Brownsville," and they said, "Have you heard of a book named Border City ?"

Well i'm in it I told them. They hire for the newspaper and the group there were all people with college education. A man that became a very close friend of mine until he died just recently in Germany. [Inaudible] Delacroix, he already had a masters, the others had at least a bachelor's, so I was in a group

that really helped me orient myself there, and I stayed after, they only kept me for another year. In 1946 I was discharged, but I got a job over there with the international branch of American Red Cross. Not the International Red Cross but the American Red Cross,

they had an international branch that work with Red Cross Societies, especially Japanese, Korean, and Chinese. I spent a year in China going around as PRO for China, and then I was in Korea, and that really broadened my horizons. When Red Cross, to use a current word, downsized,

I no longer had a job. I got a job editing a little magazine for the armed forces for two years. During that time I read a lot the section in which this magazine was also was part of the armed forces college so I took classes in the armed forces college

because I realized I didn't want to go back to Brownsville with just a junior college education, it wasn't anything for me to do that I would want to do. So I took a gamble and came to UT and I got the bachelor's and found that, of course, I was much better read than most of my fellow students.

After all, I was 35 years old, I was older than most graduate students as far as it went, so I did very well. I got what I had wanted at the time, an education, because it's necessary if you're going to really-- Let's put it in [inaudible] terms, to have a good income. [laughs]
Yes, the word heritage is synonymous with your work as a folklorist, as an activist.

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: Pardon?

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: The word heritage is synonymous with you, Dr. Paredes, as a folklorist, as an activist, as a scholar. Why is one's heritage so important?

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: That comes back to my early days when I used to listen to the old man talk and then I would spend three months over there, and then I would come, and take history, which told us a completely different view. I remember arguing with my teacher about the Monroe doctrine, some of those things

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: I got in there, because she felt that it was something very good for Latin America. I told her no, and she totally was-- I have it verbatim in the novel, she said, "What if you have a situation in which there's a little boy with candy and then a big boy comes

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: and tries to take the candy away from him, then another big boy comes and pushes the big boy away?" I said, "Yes, but then the other big boy takes the candy away from the little boy," and of course she allowed those things.

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: What we knew in our hearts from what our elders told us was different, that our heritage was not being given the respect that it deserved. What I did in The Herald during the Charro Days was milder, showing this song, for example, most Anglos I knew thought

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: that Sobre Las Olas was a German waltz, they knew it as Over The Waves. I wrote an article about Juventino Rosas and now he had written it and a number of other songs, so those things. But, more important to me was to try show what had been done, the injustices that have been done to our people,

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: and the value of our heritage. Much of that fell on deaf ears until I finally was able to come here and first I had almost forgotten the Nawa so what I did was with With His Pistol in His Hand was do the same job from a non-fiction viewpoint and using Gregorio Cortéz as a major figure in it.

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: I was not the only one on that, there were people working on that, even the LULAC at the time it was very timid, very assimilationist, yet they would come out once in a while. One of my younger brothers, Eleazar, he's dead now, he took a course-- And that was later, I never had that happen to me,

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: where he was told that, rather a textbook, said that Mexicans lived under trees and he had a big fight with the teacher, and he was expelled from school. One of my older brother went to see the LULAC people and they straightened the matter out, so that was happening all along.

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: The thing was that most of what our people knew was in corridos and in legends, in oral history, and I wanted to bring those things to the majority because I felt there were enough people of good will among the Anglos who if they saw our side they would really react. In a talk I gave

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: at Sacramento state after George Sánchez died, there was an activist. I made that point because Edward Simmons who did do a great deal, he was one of those that awakened, did say in his book The Chicano: From Caricature to Self-Portrait he says, "The lazy bandido sleeping

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: against the adobe wall finally has awakened," and I challenged him on that saying that we had been awake. People had been writing in Spanish mostly, that was the problem, so few of our elders knew English. They'd been writing, another one was Alonso Perales, En defensa de mi raza,

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: and they had been organizing and very often being put down, sometimes violently because of that. What I said there was the people that had awakened to a new ear were the Anglos because it was about that time that Anglos of good will began to see our side and we needed their help

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: to be able to make ourselves heard. That's why this story "The Hammon and the Beans" got published by an Anglo of good will, Ronnie Dugger, after a lot of Anglos refused to see that there was anything in it. We were lucky in a sense, I was, the English department has always been liberal, and Texas-

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: and they were willing to listen to me and even to allow me to get a PhD in English which was written about a ballad in Spanish about a historical figure. Chicanos very often don't know where to place me, whether to say I'm a historian, or I'm an anthropologist, or a literary figure,

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: but With His Pistol in His Hand had all of those things. I don't think I could have done that, at that time in the '50s, at any other department. Perhaps in anthropology because the anthropology have always been-- I didn't even know the anthropology department existed at that time.

tags.tag: With His Pistol in His Hand
The Chicano, From Caricature to Self-Portrait
LULAC-League of United Latin American Citizens

children.text: What kind of comments can you make to children about their abuelitos and abuelitas? Why is it so important for children to still communicate with them? Sometimes you know in modern times with so much technology we forget where part of our history is, and part of our history is in our family.
Why is it so important for them to communicate?
One of my dreams, I never fulfilled it, though there's some people that were working on it, Tony, and do you know Joe Graham?
Yes, very well, we worked together.
Yes, he got his- [video cuts to black]

- our people put documents, things they had written, books they had read, all of those things went to the castaña and I wanted my students to go and talk to their abuelitos and abuelitas, to see about what they had, and to give them to their teachers who were interested in this sort of thing,

and that has been done to some extent. Not only does it further the knowledge our people but it gives the student self-esteem, to know that just because their grandparents don't know much English, or don't know any English at all, that they're dumb.
They probably were writing décimas, or other kind of poetry, and nobody knows about it, so that I think going back to one's roots is very important.
You founded the Mexican-American Studies Department here?
Yes.
How did you feel when you became the first one that ever creates such a department?

There we had a lot of problems. They tried. We had a Dean here who was supposed to be a liberal, the only liberal thing about him was that that he hated Frank Erwin who was a member of the Board of Regents, his name was--
John Silber.
Silber, old long John Silver they called him. [laughter] He was a short man.
[laughs] Carajo.
He was a son of a bitch. [laughs]

No doubt about it, he was as racist as he could be but most of the Anglo people thought he was a liberal. After all, he was locking horns with Frank Irwin, and Frank Irwin was no raging liberal himself, obviously. He tried to hamstring this thing by pitting the blacks against us. He took charge

because he scared the administration by telling that if we didn't have an ethnic studies program immediately they would be burning in the streets, and so on. He took charge and he established a program of ethnic studies, not Mexican-American studies, not Black studies,

and he got a retired, old retired professor, a black professor to run it. Of course, he thought that he had been given the job of setting up Black studies, so I had to clash with him. For example, he wanted the program to have Swahili, all students had to learn Swahili, but Swahili was never
an African language, it was a lengua franca that the Arabs developed when they were doing slaving expeditions in east Africa. He opposed my wanting to have Spanish-- [video cuts to black]
